Not because I didn’t think law was interesting or because I doubted my intelligence. But because people like me weren’t meant for careers like this. We were the ones destined to shrink ourselves, to exist quietly and to be held back by a world that didn’t know what to do with us.  

I used to marvel at other people for simply existing. Effortlessly navigating basic things. Being human. Because growing up, I didn’t feel human. I felt like an awkward mistake. Every word, every interaction and every decision was a hurdle. I thought something was deeply wrong with me.  

A career in law was laughable. I was the opposite of everything I thought a lawyer needed to be.  

Even when I made it to law school, that belief didn’t change. Even after my autism diagnosis, which helped me reframe how I saw myself personally, I still couldn’t see myself here. I was convinced that people like me just wouldn’t make it.  

When the head of graduate recruitment from a Magic Circle firm messaged me after listening to my podcast and asked, “Have you ever considered applying” I responded instantly:  

"No, I just don’t think people like me would fit in there." 

But two years ago, I gave myself a chance. Not with confidence, but with a little belief, maybe a little delusion and a lot of frustration at the limits society had placed on me. 

Four days ago, I completed my training contract. I qualified as a solicitor.  

The last two years have tested me in ways I never expected. I’ve had to prove I belonged, navigate a system that wasn’t designed with me in mind and push through challenges that often felt relentless. The reality of being autistic in the workplace is that it is rarely easy, no matter how capable you are. 

But this autistic, introverted, highly anxious woman did it and she did it while sharing her vulnerabilities publicly and she did it well.  

I could not have done this alone:

• To my family, who are going through an incredibly difficult time but have always told me to just go for it, no matter what anyone else said;

• To my friends, who have listened to me worry about the same thing 59 times over without ever making me feel like a burden;

• To my mentors and the incredible neurodivergent community, who have given me advice, encouragement, and a reason to keep going, even on the hardest days, 

Thank you.

If you’ve ever felt like you don’t belong in this world, like there’s no space for you in the careers you dream of, although I can’t promise it will be easy, you can do it. 

Now for the next chapter 👀", "image": null, "postLink": "https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7302636938556047360?updateEntityUrn=urn%3Ali%3Afs_feedUpdate%3A%28V2%2Curn%3Ali%3Aactivity%3A7302636938556047360%29", "subTitle": null, "title": null, "type": "Post"}, {"postText": "I am absolutely honoured, humbled and so, so pleased and excited to announce that last week as a Lawyer I:

- Completely missed the point of a task because I took the instructions too literally;

- Forgot to eat or drink for nine hours straight last Thursday because I was hyper-focusing;

- Left the dishes in my sink way longer than I should have, even though I worked from home 50% of the week;

- Cried over a piece of harmless feedback;

- Had one interaction where I completely minced my words and spent the whole evening curled up trying to forget it;

- Rewrote a LinkedIn post not once, not twice, but 17 times;

- Realised I had completely blanked a member of my NDLP team for a month;

- Let a TikTok comment affect me so much that I had to come off the app for two days;

- Didn’t sleep for a whole night because I was up thinking about qualification and what comes next, but told my friend it hadn’t even crossed my mind;

- Ended up doing a task at 2 a.m. that executive dysfunction stopped me from doing during the day;

- Barely processed anything in a meeting (Amelia – 0, Transcribing software – 1);

- Missed the return period for a expensive 
jumper, so now I’m stuck with it forever;

- Slept in until noon on the public holiday, even though I was meant to set off early for a trip.

Corporate life is not always polished and perfect, especially for neurodivergent people. It's real, raw and sometimes downright messy. But that is completely fine, just no one is broadcasting this stuff. 

Sharing the highs and lows, flaws and all, because this is life, Linkedin-worthy or not.

[Photo description - Amelia (blonde hair, brown eyes) wearing a bright blue jumper, beige headphones and black jeans, taking a photo in a mirror in the lift]", "image": null, "postLink": "https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7259855854974201856?updateEntityUrn=urn%3Ali%3Afs_feedUpdate%3A%28V2%2Curn%3Ali%3Aactivity%3A7259855854974201856%29", "subTitle": null, "title": null, "type": "Post"}, {"postText": "At 13, working as a pot washer in a pub for £3 an hour, I barely said a word to anyone. Many of the staff mocked me for being “strange.”

At 16, I was asked to leave my 2 week work experience at a nursery because I “didn’t engage enough.”

From 15, I spent 3 years working in a cafe, only finding the comfort to talk with coworkers in my last year there. Another cafe job? I left after three weeks after being left to close on my own and getting so stressed. 

At 17,  I quit a job in a clothes shop after 7 weeks, having been shouted at by a customer over a security tag and struggling to connect with anyone who worked there. 

At 19, I dropped out of a vacation scheme assessment centre because the stress of making conversation on the office tour became too much. I also ran out of my first law firm open day crying. 

As a teenager, it’s safe to say I didn’t see a corporate career in my future, if any career at all. I was so disappointed in myself and just wanted to be “better.”

Looking back, I feel sad for that younger me, a literal child, and autistic, whether I knew it then or not. I was harsh on myself, but many people who were much older than me were too. I didn’t deserve that. No child does.

It took years of building confidence, finding my voice, pushing through discomfort and learning to advocate for myself to believe I could achieve anything. I was rarely told I could. I came close to losing myself, trying to be “employable” and leave that “strange” little girl behind. But she was fine. She just needed to know she wasn’t broken, she needed a little support.

Employers: be gentle with your teenage staff, apprentices and work experience students. You never know what they’re carrying and they’re still growing. Not everyone needs “toughening up.” They may just need some support and kindness.

And to the many teenage students who message me feeling the same as I did, be patient with yourself. You don’t need to have everything figured out and please don’t break yourself trying to become someone you’re not. You can’t fix neurodivergence, but you can find supportive environments, learn your strengths and work with your brain.

It feels like nothing will change at that age, but it will.

Photo: me at 13 and 13 years later ☀️

Second photo: Amelia as an adult with dark blonde hair, clear glasses, white t-shirt and red cardigan]", "image": null, "postLink": "https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7256951552169406465?updateEntityUrn=urn%3Ali%3Afs_feedUpdate%3A%28V2%2Curn%3Ali%3Aactivity%3A7256951552169406465%29", "subTitle": null, "title": null, "type": "Post"}, {"postText": "Please don’t tell me to "just go and talk to them" unless you truly understand the weight of what that entails.

Allow me to walk you through how many of my social interactions unfold as an autistic person in professional settings:

1. First, I sift through the countless negative encounters I've had over the years when meeting new people;
2. Then, anxiety creeps in about how I’ll be perceived, and I meticulously script every word I plan to say (but never am able to stick to);
3. Despite my preparation, I often come across as too blunt or awkward, fumbling over introductions or misgauging the amount of detail needed for a simple "How are you?";
4. I start the internal countdown, keeping track of how long I’ve maintained eye contact, reminding myself to look away at intervals to seem "natural";
5. My face begins to ache from forcing expressions meant to convey friendliness or enthusiasm, all in an effort to make the other person more at ease;
6. Meanwhile, I’m mentally repeating their words, trying to stay engaged and keep up with the conversation;
7. As soon as there’s a pause, panic sets in as I scramble to find a link between topics — a connection my brain doesn’t effortlessly make;
8. I either interrupt too quickly or wait too long, both of which make me seem either rude or disinterested;
9. I'm constantly working to decipher hidden meanings in gestures, expressions and tone, applying learned knowledge where instinct is lacking;
10. At some point, I’ll notice that I've been misunderstood, having focused so intensely on all the above that I can’t express myself clearly — or worse, I’ve come off as cold or rude. I know the person may now avoid me;
11. Finally, I leave the conversation drained, fully aware that I’ll spend the next several days replaying the interaction, picking apart every mistake, and berating myself.

So, I implore you: stop oversimplifying your advice to neurodivergent colleagues. What might seem like insignificant small talk to you is an entirely different ordeal for me.

A few gentle reminders for interacting with neurodivergent people:

1. Be patient, avoid jumping to conclusions, and always lead with kindness.
2. Ask people about their preferred mode of communication.
3. Consider the dynamics of that method (for example, if the conversation is virtual, some may be more comfortable with the camera off).
4. Be mindful of the environment, as sensory overload makes everything more difficult.
5. Don’t dismiss people or assume they’re not worth engaging based on a single interaction.
6. Please, don’t stare — you won’t learn much about me from my body language or eye contact alone.

#autismatwork #adhdatwork #neurodiversityatwork", "image": null, "postLink": "https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7237724263099559936?updateEntityUrn=urn%3Ali%3Afs_feedUpdate%3A%28V2%2Curn%3Ali%3Aactivity%3A7237724263099559936%29", "subTitle": null, "title": null, "type": "Post"}, {"postText": "Contrary to popular belief, you can’t “feedback” autism out of a person (or any other neurodivergence for that matter). 

I recently made a video about times when I received feedback in previous job roles that I felt was unwarranted because it targeted traits directly linked to my neurodivergence.

I didn’t think this would be particularly controversial, but given the amount of hate comments, it’s clear that some people don’t and may never agree with me.

So here is how I would have responded:

You can tell me to be more social or to communicate in a more neurotypical way—but all that will do is exhaust me through masking. And when I’m masking, my other work will suffer. I already fulfill all the necessary social aspects of my role. What I take issue with is the “extra” stuff that isn’t part of my job description and doesn’t contribute directly to my performance.

You can tell me to process things faster—but I can’t physically change the way my brain works. I already employ strategies like transcribing instructions, taking notes, and asking clarifying questions to process information as quickly as possible. I also can’t make up for poor instruction giving. 

You can tell me to stop getting overwhelmed—but without the right support, and when I’m constantly placed in situations that clash with my neurodivergent traits, I can’t manage that overwhelm. I already attend weekly therapy and have worked for years to maintain your version of “professionalism.” So, if you see me overwhelmed, it’s because I’ve been pushed too far. Also, I will never regulate my emotions in the same way a neurotypical person would, because I’m not neurotypical. I also can’t fully control sensory overload. I do everything I can to limit my exposure, but sensory input isn’t always avoidable.

When I receive feedback like this, what it tells me is that while you’ll employ me as a neurodivergent person, you still expect me to change everything that makes me neurodivergent—for your convenience. I’m not ashamed of who I am, but this feedback makes me feel like I should be.

People like to point out that these traits are all mandatory parts of most jobs. But a little open-mindedness would show you that 1) I can still do most jobs, especially if I’m given the support I’m legally entitled to (and no, that doesn’t make me “entitled”) and 2) neurodivergent people, including myself, need to be seen holistically. Yes, I may struggle in some areas, but I excel in others. And while I hate having to justify my challenges by listing my strengths, none of the issues I’ve mentioned are blanket “weaknesses”—there is nuance to all of them.

I am not immune to constructive feedback, I have areas I need to develop on and I will in every role I have. I work hard to work on feedback I receive. But what I won’t accept is feedback that implies my neurodivergence is something to be fixed.

•	Trade credit insurance
•	Financial guaranty insurance law issues
•	Regulatory compliance analysis communication with regulators

I’m proud to be at the forefront of development of the credit insurance market in the United States, having negotiated hundreds of policies and assisted with the growth of the market in recent years. I have co-authored a standardized industry template for trade credit transactions published by the International Trading & Forfaiting Association (ITFA) and speak regularly at industry conferences, such as for the International Association of Credit Portfolio Managers.

I’m also fortunate to maintain an active pro bono practice, including leading a 50-state survey for Start Small Think Big on benefits and resources available to veteran-owned businesses.

#legal #finance #biglaw #newyork #lawyer #lawfirm", "image": null, "postLink": "https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:6959973150704947200?updateEntityUrn=urn%3Ali%3Afs_feedUpdate%3A%28V2%2Curn%3Ali%3Aactivity%3A6959973150704947200%29", "subTitle": null, "title": null, "type": "Post"}, {"postText": "Stanford helped me embrace my inner tortoise.

Last week I graduated from Stanford Law School's Master of Laws (LL.M.) in Corporate Governance & Practice. 

As I gathered my thoughts throughout the emotional whirlwind of Graduation Week, two main ideas kept standing out in my mind: i) do not lose sight of your goals; and ii) failures/struggles are part of your process.

It was Reed Hastings, CEO of Netflix, who told us at Commencement to look around at our classmates and recognize that some would leave Stanford University as hares while others would be more like tortoises.

Internally, I had struggled with this concept for many years.

At 17, my plans to study abroad out of highschool crumbled due to anxiety. I bargained with myself to someday pursue a Masters in exchange.

At 18, I struggled to chose a career, finally opting for a law degree at the Facultad de Derecho, Universidad de Buenos Aires; although I loved the law, I was certain I would never want to be an attorney.

At 24, I entered Marval O'Farrell, Mairal, still convinced that lawyering wasn't for me. It was the law firm who nurtured my interests and showed me the potentiality of a career at Banking & Finance.

At 26, I saw my peers pursuing their LL.M. This was an opportunity for me to dust-off my teenage dreams while furthering my professional career. I soon found out I was in over my head.

At 27, I decided to postpone my applications. I struggled with impostor syndrome and felt I would never be accepted. It was through the unconditional support and reassurance of my family, friends, tutors and partner, that I recouped myself and worked on recognizing my potentiality.

At 29, I arrived at Stanford University, clueless, worried and doubting my every step. It was Dean Jenny Martinez's words at Orientation that strung a chord within me. We had made it. Remind yourself of all your hard work. It is extremely easy to get lost. Don't lose focus on why you came.

Now, at 30, I graduated from Stanford Law School with a dream, a chip off my shoulders, and a lesson. You are never too late, you are just on time. Do not lose focus, life will always give you chances to redeem yourself and revisit "lost" opportunities. 

As I prepare to sit for the NYS Bar Exam, and move to NY to join the Finance team at Willkie Farr & Gallagher LLP, I just want to thank some people that helped me get here.

Thank you to my family, friends, partner and colleagues for supporting this stubborn tortoise in pursuing his dreams.

Thank you to my Stanford classmates, my new cardinal family, who elected me as their AD President for the Class of 2022. I have never felt as appreciated and recognized as I did by you.

Thank you to Stanford University, Andres Mena, Laura Rupenian, Hernan Slemenson and Willkie Farr & Gallagher LLP for nurturing this potential hare and allowing me to keep on dreaming.

Words cannot express how grateful I am for this past year.

This has forever changed me.

#llm #stanforduniversity #lawschool", "image": null, "postLink": "https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:6945044309318529025?updateEntityUrn=urn%3Ali%3Afs_feedUpdate%3A%28V2%2Curn%3Ali%3Aactivity%3A6945044309318529025%29", "subTitle": null, "title": null, "type": "Post"}, {"postText": "Today, after 6 years at Marval O'Farrell Mairal, I find myself closing one of the most exciting and challenging phases in my professional career. Being part of their Banking & Finance department has allowed me to discover what kind of lawyer I aim to be and understand my aspirations as a diverse leading professional coming from an emerging economy such as Argentina.
 
Next month I will be joining Stanford Law School in order to pursue my Master of Laws (LL.M.) degree and continue to explore the disruptive nature of the Fintech industry in the very heart of Silicon Valley. I can only hope to acquire the necessary tools to continue to facilitate the digital transformation and symbiotic integration of financial institutions with Fintech solutions.
 
Thank you Stanford University for allowing me to continue to dream and providing me with this life-changing opportunity.
 
I would like to acknowledge all the professionals I have met ever who have made my work life so enjoyable and have shared their knowledge and skills with me; I will not take it for granted.

A special thank you to my recommenders: Patricia Lopez Aufranc, Cecilia Mairal, Pablo Gayol, CFA, Santiago Padua, CFA and Santiago Juan Cornes Sangiao; and mentors: Hernan Slemenson, Gabriel Matarasso, María Pamela Nohra and Milagros Abelenda.
 
Ever since we are young, we are taught to dream big but it is not often we are told about the effort and sacrifices this will entail. If there is something I have learned from this next step in my career is to measure success not by the number of opportunities you had, but by the number of opportunities you sought after.
 
If by any chance you come across this post and think I can ever be of any help to you, please do not hesitate to reach out.
 
Be proud.

"employmentType": "Full-time"}], "updates": [{"postText": "Making a relatively rare Linkedin post to share two recent professional developments. 

First, I graduated last month with cum laude academic honors from Harvard Law School, a feat that on its own merits immense pride and gratitude. However, the diploma itself says nothing of the many connections I forged, projects I had undertaken, and organizations I helped lead during my time at HLS. It is of those experiences that I am most proud and grateful.

For example, and secondly, my Student Note, "Money Talks, and the Law Won't Listen: The Fatal Consequences of Discriminatory Corruption for Women in Mexico and Bolivia," was recently published by the Harvard Human Rights Journal. It is available to read here: https://lnkd.in/d3v6CGqK 

This Note is the culmination of my dedication these past three years to illuminating the myriad legal issues involving gender-based violence and gender identity, particularly in Latin America. 

In this Note, I examine the tragic injustice of feminicide against the backdrop of the widespread corruption driving its sustained endurance. I observe that the corruption involved with feminicide is a self-perpetuating cycle—corruption begets violent attitudes towards women, and such violent attitudes beget further corruption. The result of this cyclical pattern is a law enforcement apparatus whose attitude towards feminicide is apathetic at best and complicit at worst.

La sentencia de instancia había desestimado la demanda de conflicto colectivo planteada por el comité de empresa interesando la aplicación de un único convenio colectivo en un centro de trabajo en el que se venían aplicando dos convenios colectivos de distinto ámbito funcional. La Sala desestima el recurso por entender que el convenio colectivo cuya aplicación se solicitaba no era el de la actividad principal de la empresa demandada y que la aplicación de dos convenios colectivos diferenciados en el centro de trabajo no obedecía a motivos discriminatorios. 

Tras un breve análisis del estado de la cuestión en materia de determinación del convenio colectivo aplicable cuando se realizan varias actividades diferenciadas en el seno de una misma empresa o centro de trabajo, se reflexiona sobre los criterios a tener en cuenta para la determinación del convenio colectivo aplicable y las dificultades interpretativas que puede acarrear la Sentencia en sede de ejecución.
